data.description:
# P2.1 — Hybrid Logical Clock for event ordering (skew-immune replay)

**Correctness backbone.** Closes the one architectural gap a distributed tracker
should not ship without; P2.3 rides its total order.

## Context (problem + research)

rebar orders replay by the `${timestamp_ns}` filename prefix; invariant I8 admits
EDIT/COMMENT interleaving is "best-effort" under clock skew (only STATUS forks are
skew-independent, UUID-keyed at `reducer/_processors.py:78-120`). Two agents on
skewed clocks editing the same field resolve by last *wall-clock* writer -> silent
clobber. **git-bug solves exactly this with a persisted Lamport clock**
(`util/lamport/*`, `clocks/<name>`): a single `uint64`, advanced monotonically,
**re-seeded by witnessing max over history**; the local file is a disposable
cache, the authoritative value rides in git. Replay orders by logical time then a
content-id tiebreak. Radicle uses the same family (causal DAG + actor-id tiebreak).

## Evidence (experiments)

- EXP1: `time_ns()` is 19 digits until ~year 2286; equal-width ints sort identically
  as strings and ints; string sort only breaks across DIFFERING widths -> stay 19-digit.
- EXP4: 2400 concurrent flock'd `next_tick()` ticks — all unique/monotonic/19-digit.
- EXP4b: with **no cache file**, `next_tick_for_ticket()` still issues a tick >
  `max(prefix)`, 19-digit -> the cache is reconstructable (git-bug's model).
- EXP-jq: the prefix is >2^53; jq rounds it -> **no jq/arithmetic may ever touch it**
  (reinforces P1.0's one-Python-serializer rule).
- EXP-R2 (real code): `event_sort_key` ts segment is `str` today; int-order ==
  string-order on real filenames; malformed names take the fallback -> change is safe.

## What / Implementation detail

1. New `rebar._store.hlc.next_tick()` = single monotonic integer
   `max(hlc.state, max(prefix of TARGET TICKET's events after sync), time_ns()) + 1`,
   RMW under a **local** `.rebar/hlc.lock` (held only inside the call -> no ordering
   hazard with the write lock). `.rebar/hlc.state` is a **disposable cache**
   (gitignored, I3/I7); correctness is re-derived from the durable log, so a
   missing/stale/raced cache is still correct.
2. Thread it through the four live timestamp seams: `_commands/_seam.py:153`,
   `ticket_txn.py` (STATUS/claim), reconciler `inbound_translate`, and
   `ticket-compact.sh` (via `python3 -m rebar._store.hlc next`). Add an injectable
   `REBAR_HLC_NOW` for the skewed-clock test.
3. Change `reducer/_sort.py:event_sort_key` (line 21) to `int(ts_segment)` (malformed
   fallback), keeping the `(ts, type_order, name)` tuple + LINK<UNLINK tiebreak;
   align the other prefix sorts (`graph/_links.py:53`, `_commands/unlink.py:47`,
   bare `sorted()` STATUS scans at `ticket_txn.py:187/318`). Keep the UUID tiebreak
   (git-bug: logical-time then content-id).
4. Bump `reducer/_version.py:SCHEMA_VERSION` -> 2. Single-integer encoding keeps
   width at 19 digits, so older clones still string-compare correctly.

## Success Criteria

- A NEW skewed-clock regression (two clones, injected skew) shows EDIT/COMMENT
  converge to the same value AND order respects causality — this test does not
  exist today and is the executable proof the gap is closed.
- Width-comparator test: legacy 19-digit and new names sort into one global order.
- v1-reducer replay of a v2 store still yields a valid state (forward-compat).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `rebar._store.hlc.next_tick()` (single-int, local-lock RMW, witness max(prefix), injectable clock)
- [ ] threaded through all four live timestamp seams (incl. bash via `python3 -m`)
- [ ] `event_sort_key` + the 3 other prefix sorts use integer comparison; UUID tiebreak kept
- [ ] SCHEMA_VERSION -> 2; event-schema.md updated
- [ ] NEW skewed-clock EDIT/COMMENT convergence regression (merged red-first), width-comparator + forward-compat tests
- [ ] staged behind `REBAR_HLC` with kill-switch for one release

See `docs/remediation-implementation-plan.md` (P2.1).
data.title: P2.1 — Hybrid Logical Clock for event ordering (skew-immune replay)
